features.summary: NVIDIA reported strong Q1 fiscal 2021 results with revenue of $3.08 billion, up 39% year-over-year. Data center revenue hit a record $1.14 billion (up 80% YoY), while gaming revenue was $1.34 billion (up 27% YoY). The company completed its Mellanox acquisition in April and launched the new Ampere A100 GPU architecture, which contributed meaningfully to Q1 revenue despite just being announced.
features.management_outlook: Management expects Q2 revenue of $3.65 billion (plus or minus 2%), with Mellanox contributing in the low teens percentage range. They anticipate automotive revenue to decline 40% sequentially due to COVID-19 impacts, while gaming is expected to grow sequentially in low-to-mid single digits. The company sees strong demand for A100 products and maintains solid visibility into Q2 data center business, though they guide only one quarter at a time due to macro uncertainties.
features.risks: Key risks include ongoing COVID-19 impacts on automotive and retail channels, potential trade tensions with China affecting high-performance computing systems, and the challenge of managing through economic reopening uncertainties. The automotive industry faces significant disruption, and there are concerns about potential CapEx digestion cycles in data centers, though management believes current conditions differ from previous cycles due to greater workload diversity and cloud acceleration.
features.key_drivers: Major growth drivers include the structural shift to cloud computing and AI adoption, the launch of Ampere architecture with 20x performance improvements, and expansion into edge AI applications. The company benefits from RTX momentum in gaming with 33 games in the pipeline, accelerated work-from-home trends boosting gaming and professional visualization demand, and the integration of Mellanox enabling end-to-end data center solutions. COVID-19 is accelerating digital transformation and creating new opportunities in computational defense systems and robotics.
